BBC Culture
Avatar: Fire and Ash review – a stunning, if familiar, return to Pandora
By Caryn James
Culture correspondent
15 December 2025
Share
Comments
Image caption,
The film returns to the world of Pandora
Avatar: Fire and Ash is the third instalment in James Cameron’s epic sci‑fi franchise, and it arrives with the weight of expectation that comes with being one of the most expensive films ever made. It is also, perhaps, the most visually spectacular.
The story picks up years after the events of Avatar: The Way of Water, with Jake Sully (Sam Worthington) and Neytiri (Zoe Saldaña) now leading the Na’vi resistance against the continued human colonisation of Pandora. Their children have grown, and new threats emerge from both the human forces and within Pandora itself.
Cameron once again pushes the boundaries of visual effects, with breathtaking underwater sequences and aerial battles that are nothing short of jaw‑dropping. The 3D is immersive, the motion‑capture performances are nuanced, and the world‑building remains unparalleled.
Yet for all its technical mastery, Fire and Ash struggles to escape the shadow of its predecessors. The plot follows a familiar arc – human greed versus ecological harmony – and some of the emotional beats feel recycled. The film’s lengthy runtime (over three hours) may test the patience of those not fully invested in the mythology.
Still, there is much to admire. The action set‑pieces are thrilling, the score by Simon Franglen is stirring, and the central performances are committed. For fans of the franchise, this will be a satisfying, if not groundbreaking, return to Pandora.
For everyone else, it is a dazzling spectacle that sometimes forgets to have a heart.
Avatar: Fire and Ash is in cinemas now.
